title: Phonon polariton confinement in isotopically pure MOVPE-grown BN triangles
authors: Maximilian Scharpey, Oskar Schröer, Daniel Wigger, Lena Miler, Jakub Iwański, Andrzej Wysmołek, Johannes Binder, Iris Niehues
abstract: Phonon polaritons, quasiparticles formed by the resonant hybridization of light and lattice vibrations, exhibit unique properties like the possibility of hyperbolic dispersions. In this context, exfoliated hexagonal boron nitride (hBN) has emerged as a promising material for phonon polariton-based research. However, to advance toward practical applications, it is essential to demonstrate efficient phonon-polariton propagation and confinement in large-area epitaxial BN. To address this topic, we use metalorganic vapor phase epitaxy (MOVPE)-grown BN and investigate the phonon polariton properties using scattering-type scanning near-field optical microscopy (s-SNOM) and nanoscale Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (nano-FTIR). We report remarkably long phonon polariton propagation lengths, indicating the high crystalline quality of the BN layer. By using epitaxially grown isotopically pure triangular islands, we further demonstrate efficient phonon-polariton confinement with mode patterns tunable by the incident light wavelength. Our results pave the way for implementing all-epitaxial, microscale, high-quality polariton resonators for nanophotonics and quantum optics.
summary: The research investigates phonon polaritons in epitaxial boron nitride (BN) films, moving from bulk characterization to engineered microstructures. By combining s-SNOM and nano-FTIR measurements on isotopically pure ${}^{10}	ext{BN}$ triangles, the authors successfully demonstrated highly confined polariton modes. This work paves the way for building scalable, high-quality nanophotonic devices.
main_problem: The primary goal is to demonstrate efficient phonon-polariton propagation and confinement within large-area, epitaxial boron nitride (BN) structures for advanced nanophotonics applications.
main_result: The authors successfully demonstrated high-quality polariton propagation in MOVPE-grown natural BN and achieved clear evidence of mode confinement using isotopically pure ${}^{10}	ext{BN}$ triangular islands.
method: Measurements combine scattering-type s-SNOM and nano-FTIR spectroscopy, analyzed using complex near-field optical signals and fitting to theoretical interference models.
model_or_system: The system involves BN thin films grown by MOVPE, specifically utilizing isotopically pure ${}^{10}	ext{BN}$ triangular islands on a natural BN layer. The physics relies on the resonant hybridization of light and lattice vibrations (phonon polaritons).
key_observables: Phonon polariton dispersion relation ($\omega$ vs. $q$), complex near-field amplitude and phase maps, and spectral features from s-SNOM/nano-FTIR.
important_parameters: Wavenumbers like $	ext{TO}$ and $	ext{LO}$ modes (e.g., $1354 	ext{ cm}^{-1}$, $1608 	ext{ cm}^{-1}$), island size, and epitaxial growth quality.
main_assumptions: The analysis often assumes straight wavefronts near edges for initial measurements, and the comparison between s-SNOM and nano-FTIR requires complex geometric alignment algorithms.
figures_summary: Figures show s-SNOM characterization of MOVPE-grown BN (including topography, amplitude/phase maps), schematics of ${}^{10}	ext{BN}$ island structures, and plots comparing measured vs. simulated phonon polariton dispersion relations.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from characterizing high-quality bulk-like propagation in natural BN to demonstrating controlled confinement within engineered micro-triangular islands using advanced near-field optical techniques.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it pushes the boundaries of solid-state photonics by creating tunable, all-epitaxial polariton resonators, which are crucial components for quantum light sources and detectors.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Phonon polariton characterization of MOVPE grown natBN. (a) AFM topography map of the sample also showing natBN tri- angles (see Figure 2). (b) AFM line scan across the edge of the natBN layer. (c) s-SNOM amplitude 𝑠4 (fourth demodulation or- der) of the sample area from (a) for an excitation wavenumber of 1480 cm−1. (d) s-SNOM phase map 𝜑4 corresponding to (c). (e) Complex valued s-SNOM signal ¯𝐸nf (dark green dots) as function of polariton propagation distance 𝑥vertically averaged over the rect- angle in (c, d). Fit (green line) according to Eq. (2) with resulting 𝑞= 1.45 × 105 cm−1. (f) Measured phonon polariton dispersion (green dots) with theoretical fit (light green line)...

Figure 2. Isotopically pure 10BN triangular islands. (a) Schematic of the sample structure consisting of a MOVPE-grown natBN layer with isotopically pure triangular islands (10BN) transferred on a Si/SiO2 substrate. (b) AFM topography image and (c) extracted line scan of the investigated area. The isotopically pure 10BN triangles with a thickness of about 10 nm are grown on a 10 nm thick natBN layer.

Figure 3. Confined polariton modes in 10BN triangular islands. Upper two rows: s-SNOM 𝑠3 amplitude images (3rd demodulation order) taken at selected excitation wavenumbers increasing from (a) to (e) and corresponding simulations. High intensities are shown in red and low intensities in blue colors. All images are initially normalized to the respective Si/SiO2 substrate value and finally to their respective maximum value. Lower two rows: corresponding s-SNOM phase 𝜑3 images again normalized to the Si/SiO2 substrate and simulations. The outline of the 10BN triangle is marked in (a) by the dashed black line.
Figure 4. Phonon polariton dispersion of the 10BN triangle de- termined from the confined modes in Figure 3. The resulting phonon wavenumbers are 𝜔TO,𝑥= (1393 ± 6) cm−1 and 𝜔LO,𝑥= (1621 ± 7) cm−1.

Figure 5. s-SNOM and nano-FTIR spectroscopy of confined polariton modes. (a) AFM topography image of the 10BN triangular island (same as Figure 2(b)). Positions where spectra are taken are marked by colored dots. (b-c) nano-FTIR spectra (amplitude 𝑠3 top and phase 𝜑3 bottom, solid black lines) taken at the positions marked in (a) together with the extracted s-SNOM values from the s-SNOM images in Figures 3 and SI (colored dots). The gray vertical lines indicate the excitation wavenumbers corresponding to the s-SNOM maps shown in 3 and the arrows mark distinct maxima (↑) and minima (↓) at the respective position (colors) in the measured maps.
